title: Chest X-Ray Multi-Task Research
subtitle: Classification and segmentation module lead, contributing to a 5-module clinical chest X-ray platform.
reflection: TorchXRayVision DenseNet121 is a well-validated medical baseline. I learned that using an established model in a new domain requires understanding why it was built that way, not just what it outputs.
I trained both DenseNet and DeiT on the same data. DenseNet generalized better. The convolutional inductive bias matters more than I expected at this dataset size.
DICOM files are not just images. Window level, pixel spacing, and photometric interpretation all change what the model sees. I learned this by getting it wrong first.
Owning a module in a team project means the interface is as important as the internals. Other people's code breaks if my endpoint changes.
Packaging AI as a microservice is a different mindset. The model is not the product. The endpoint is.

milestones.role: Researcher · Classification and Segmentation Module Lead
highlights: Led two of five AI modules within the research team: pathology classification and organ segmentation.

Built a 14-class pathology classifier for chest X-rays, validated on VinBig and NIH ChestX-ray14, by fine-tuning an established medical imaging model and comparing it against a transformer-based alternative.
Built an organ segmentation module covering right lung, left lung, and heart: annotated masks and overlays from DICOM inputs, packaged as a microservice shared with the full team.
Both modules served the team's web and desktop apps through shared API endpoints.
Part of a 5-module system covering classification, segmentation, report generation, VQA, and visual grounding.
